name: bash-dev
description: Bash scripting development standards, error handling, and best practices. Activated when working with .sh files or Bash scripts.
capabilities: Generate Bash scripts with proper shebang and error handling flags
Apply set -euo pipefail for robust error detection
Implement safe variable quoting and local variable scoping
Create comprehensive function documentation with examples
Validate user input and function arguments
Follow ShellCheck recommendations for script quality
author: Cain96
securitySummary: This is a pure documentation skill containing only markdown guidelines and example code patterns for Bash development. No executable code, scripts, network calls, or file system access. The static scanner flagged 59 patterns as dangerous, but ALL are false positives: (1) the file has 319 lines but scanner referenced lines up to 311+ which don't exist, (2) documentation patterns like 'command -v' and heredoc syntax were misidentified as shell execution, (3) ShellCheck directives (# shellcheck) were flagged as C2 keywords, (4) no actual cryptographic algorithms or malicious code exists. The content promotes secure coding practices including proper error handling, variable quoting, and input validation.
valueStatement: Bash scripts often fail silently or expose security vulnerabilities. This skill provides comprehensive standards for error handling, safe variable usage, and reliable script structure that prevent common mistakes.
userTitle: Write secure Bash scripts with best practices
limitations: Does not execute or test scripts directly
Does not provide runtime debugging of running scripts
Does not install or manage shell environments or dependencies

useCases.title: Reliable automation scripts
useCases.description: Create robust automation scripts for deployment pipelines and system administration tasks.

useCases.title: Build and deployment scripts
useCases.description: Write safe build scripts that handle errors gracefully and provide clear output.

useCases.title: System maintenance scripts
useCases.description: Develop maintenance scripts that fail safely and avoid causing system damage.

promptTemplates.title: New script
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a Bash script that does X. Include proper shebang, error handling with set -euo pipefail, and function documentation.

promptTemplates.title: Fix script
promptTemplates.prompt: Review this Bash script for security issues and add proper error handling, variable quoting, and input validation.

promptTemplates.title: Pattern
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how to safely implement X in Bash (where X is file operations, array handling, or command execution) with proper error handling.

promptTemplates.title: Best practice
promptTemplates.prompt: What are the best practices for writing secure and maintainable Bash scripts? Include examples of proper quoting, local variables, and error handling.

bestPractices: Always use set -euo pipefail at the script start to catch errors early
Quote all variables with "${var}" to prevent word splitting and globbing
Use local variables inside functions to prevent unintended side effects
antiPatterns: Using unquoted variables like cd $HOME/dir which can fail on spaces
Skipping error handling with mkdir /some/dir without checking the result
Omitting set options which allows silent failures and undefined variables

faq.question: Does this skill work on Windows with WSL?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill follows POSIX standards that work in WSL, Git Bash, and Cygwin environments.

faq.question: What error handling flags are recommended?
faq.answer: Use set -euo pipefail. This exits on error, undefined variables, and pipeline failures.

faq.question: Can I use this with shellcheck?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill follows ShellCheck-compatible patterns and includes integration examples.

faq.question: Is my data safe when using this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. This is a prompt-based skill that only generates code. It does not access or store any files.

faq.question: Why do my scripts fail silently?
faq.answer: Without set -euo pipefail, Bash continues past errors. The skill teaches proper error handling to prevent this.

faq.question: How is this different from auto-generated scripts?
faq.answer: This skill provides context-aware guidance with proper documentation, error handling, and security considerations.
